question: What % of US adults will develop a mental illness during their lifetime
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
AMERICA'S
GUN VIOLENCE
EPIDEMIC

Although gun violence in America is frequently attributed to the availability of firearms, another relevant factor is our nation's broken mental health care system. Although most people who suffer from mental health disorders are nonviolent, severe mental health disorders that go untreated can increase the risk for violence. Thus, if we hope to curb gun violence, we must de-stigmatize and treat mental health disorders.

GUN VIOLENCE IN AMERICA

2/3 OF ALL MASS KILLINGS INVOLVE GUNS.

There were 154 mass killings from 2006 to 2010.

In America, a mass killing — defined as having 4 victims, not including the killer — occurs once every 2 weeks.

THOSE INCLUDE
774 VICTIMS

OF THOSE VICTIMS,
161 WERE CHILDREN

GUNS & SUICIDE

31,000
GUN RELATED DEATHS IN THE UNITED STATES

5% OF ALL SUICIDE ATTEMPTS ARE BY GUNSHOT.

19,000 OF THOSE ARE SELF INFLICTED

85% OF THOSE ATTEMPTS RESULTED IN DEATH.

Suicide is the 11th leading cause of death in the United States and the third-leading cause of death for people ages 10–24 years.

In July 2007, a nationwide report indicated that male veterans are twice as likely to die by suicide as compared to their civilian peers.

ACCESS TO GUNS

FEDERAL GUN LAWS CURRENTLY ENACTED INCLUDE:

- Prohibition of firearms sales or transfers to those suffering from mental health disorders and others

- Federal background checks on firearms purchases from licensed sellers; records of which must legally be destroyed after 24 hours

- Prohibition of firearms possession in school zones, unless stored in a locked container

- Prohibition of fully automatic weapon sales to civilians

- Prohibition of possession of a firearm by persons convicted of felonies

- Prohibition of carrying a handgun without a permit in some states

BUT GUN CONTROL IS LARGELY DICTATED BY INDIVIDUAL STATES, CREATING LARGE DISCREPANCIES BETWEEN STATES. FOR EXAMPLE, SOUTH DAKOTA HAS SOME OF THE WEAKEST GUN LAWS AND CALIFORNIA HAS SOME OF THE STRONGEST.

SOUTH
DAKOTA
- No law authorizing the reporting of mental health adjudications to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS)
- No license to purchase firearms
- No waiting period on firearm purchases
- No restrictions on concealed carry permit
- No magazine capacity restriction
- No firearm registration

CALIFORNIA
- Gun dealers must be licensed
- Waiting period and background checks required for all handgun purchases
- Assault weapons are restricted
- Magazine capacity restrictions
- Gun shows require licensed dealers
- Permit required to carry concealed weapons
- No open carry

Gun control laws are further weakened by discrepancies in regulations for licensed firearms dealers, which are stricter, and regulations for private sales, which are more lax.

MENTAL HEALTH AND GUN VIOLENCE

7 MILLION
Americans suffer from severe mental health disorders, such as schizophrenia, acute depression or bipolar disorder.

The increased risk of violence for most mentally ill people is minimal, but when paired with issues like substance abuse and left untreated, the risk is more than doubled.

HALF OF U.S. ADULTS WILL DEVELOP A MENTAL ILLNESS DURING THEIR LIFETIME.

People with severe mental illness are roughly twice as likely to commit violent acts in their lifetime.

THE FLOUNDERING MENTAL HEALTH SYSTEM

LESS THAN HALF OF AMERICANS WITH SEVERE MENTAL HEALTH ISSUES RECEIVE ADEQUATE TREATMENT.

$1.6 BILLION in state mental health services have been cut since 2009.

These cuts led to significant reductions in hospital and community services for those with serious mental illness, including cutbacks to:
HOUSING
OUTPATIENT INTERVENTIONS
MEDICATION
CRISIS SERVICES

13%
of children ages 8 to 15 live with severe mental health disorders

BUT ONLY

20%
of those children receive treatment

ONE-HALF OF ALL LIFETIME CASES OF MENTAL ILLNESS BEGIN BY AGE 14. YET THERE ARE LONG DELAYS — SOMETIMES DECADES — BETWEEN THE ONSET OF SYMPTOMS AND WHEN PEOPLE SEEK AND RECEIVE TREATMENT.

500,000:
TOTAL BEDS AVAILABLE IN AMERICAN MENTAL INSTITUTIONS AT PEAK LEVELS IN THE 1950s

43,000:
TOTAL BEDS CURRENTLY AVAILABLE

This shortage of inpatient psychiatric care makes finding appropriate care difficult, if not impossible.

IN 2011
24 STATES
SLASHED MENTAL HEALTH PROVIDER REIMBURSEMENT RATES FOR MEDICAID — THE MOST IMPORTANT SOURCE OF FUNDING OF PUBLIC MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES.

REAL
SOLUTIONS

"We must not only tackle gun control but we must also fix deficiencies in the U.S. mental health system, gaps that can leave many people with serious mental illnesses and family members without an easy way to get the treatment and support we know can help avert catastrophes."
Lynn R. Goldman, pediatrician and dean of the George Washington University School of Public Health and Health Services

New Assault Weapons Ban
Senator Dianne Feinstein has authored the Assault Weapons Ban of 2013, which would ban 157 military-style weapons and large-capacity magazines.

Funding Mental Health Care
The recently passed Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act will expand Medicaid coverage and include requirements to cover mental health care. It will also increase federal support for community-based services and require parity for mental health benefits.

Gun Violence Research
On January 16, President Barack Obama signed an executive order directing the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to research causes of gun violence and preventative measures.

Destigmatizing Mental Illness
On February 4, Department of Health & Human Services Secretary Kathleen Sebelius announced an initiative to end discrimination against people with mental illnesses, expand access to care, and to integrate mental health with primary care to help people seek help and keep people from seeking care.

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"
The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"
The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"
Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

question: which touch screen is more durable - resistive or infrared?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW DOES A TOUCHSCREEN PHONE WORK?

TECHNOLOGY

There are three types of technologies widely used in touch screen phones today: resistive, capacitive, and infrared.

RESISTIVE

PHONES
Samsung Messager Touch, Samsung Instinct, HTC Touch Diamond, LG Dare

HOW IT WORKS
Small dots separate multiple layers of material that are transmitting currents. When the top flexible layer is pushed into the lower layer, the electrical current changes and the location of impact is calculated.

COST TO MAKE
$

SCREEN MATERIAL
A layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass.

TOOL FOR INPUT
Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY
Poor visibility

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?
No

DURABILITY
Fair Durability. Screens are vulnerable to scratches and other minor damage. The screen will wear over time and need replacing.

CAPACITIVE

PHONES
Huawei Ascend, Sanyo Zio, Apple's iPhone, HTC Hero, DROID Eris, Palm Pre, Blackberry Storm

HOW IT WORKS
The current is broadcast from the corners of the screen. When a finger touches the screen, it changes the current, and that location is calculated.

COST TO MAKE
$$

SCREEN MATERIAL
Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT
Bare fingers only

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY
Good visibility

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?
Yes

DURABILITY
Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

INFRARED
(HEAT-SENSITIVE AND OPTICAL)

PHONES
Samsung U600 (heat), Neonode N2 (optical)

HOW IT WORKS
HEAT-SENSITIVE
Heat-sensitive must be touched with a warm object to register point of impact.

OPTICAL
Optical uses sensors to shoot a grid of invisible beams just above the screen. Point of impact is calculated based upon the point where the x-y axis is interrupted.

COST TO MAKE
$$$

SCREEN MATERIAL
Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT
Optical: Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus
Heat-Sensitive: Warm, bare fingers

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY
Good visibility, but strong ambient light impacts productivity and precision.

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?
Yes

DURABILITY
Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

AMERICA'S
GUN VIOLENCE EPIDEMIC

Although gun violence in America is frequently attributed to the availability of firearms, another relevant factor is our nation's broken mental health care system. Although most people who suffer from mental health disorders are nonviolent, severe mental health disorders that go untreated can increase the risk for violence. Thus, if we hope to curb gun violence, we must de-stigmatize and treat mental health disorders.

GUN VIOLENCE IN AMERICA

2/3 OF ALL MASS KILLINGS INVOLVE GUNS.

In America, a mass killing — defined as having 4 victims, not including the killer — occurs once every 2 weeks.

There were 154 mass killings from 2006 to 2010.

THOSE INCLUDE
774 VICTIMS

OF THOSE VICTIMS,
161 WERE CHILDREN

GUNS & SUICIDE

31,000
GUN RELATED DEATHS IN THE UNITED STATES

19,000 OF THOSE ARE SELF INFLICTED

5% OF ALL SUICIDE ATTEMPTS ARE BY GUNSHOT.

85% OF THOSE ATTEMPTS RESULTED IN DEATH.

Suicide is the 11th leading cause of death in the United States and the third leading cause of death for people ages 15-24 years.

In July 2007, a nationwide report indicated that male veterans are twice as likely to die by suicide as compared to their civilian peers.

ACCESS TO GUNS
FEDERAL GUN LAWS CURRENTLY ENACTED INCLUDE:

- Prohibition of firearm sales or transfers to those suffering from mental health disorders and other issues.
- Prohibition of firearms possession in school zones, unless licensed by a person under federal law or unless the firearm is unloaded and in a locked container.
- Prohibition of possession by felons and some domestic violence offenders.
- Federal background checks on firearms purchases from licensed dealers; records of checks are generally kept.
- Some limits on who may purchase handguns and limits on interstate transfers.
- Prohibition of fully automatic weapon sales to civilians without special registration and tax stamp.

BUT GUN CONTROL IS LARGELY DICTATED BY INDIVIDUAL STATES, CREATING LARGE DISCREPANCIES BETWEEN STATES. FOR EXAMPLE, SOUTH DAKOTA HAS SOME OF THE WEAKEST GUN LAWS AND CALIFORNIA HAS SOME OF THE STRONGEST:

SOUTH DAKOTA
- No law authorizing the reporting of mental health information to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS)
- No permit required to purchase a handgun
- No requirement to register firearms
- No magazine capacity restriction
- Concealed carry permitted and concealed carry permit reciprocity with many states

CALIFORNIA
- State laws to track and disarm people adjudicated mentally ill
- Universal background checks for most firearm purchases
- Licensing and registration requirements for handguns
- Restriction on magazine capacities
- Prohibits possession of firearms by some categories of mentally ill persons

Gun control laws are further weakened by discrepancies in regulations for licensed firearms dealers, which are stricter, and regulations for private sales, which are more lax.

MENTAL HEALTH AND GUN VIOLENCE

7 MILLION
Americans suffer from severe mental health issues, such as schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, or major depression.

The increased risk of violence for most mentally ill people is minimal, but when paired with substance abuse or left untreated, the risk is more than doubled.

HALF OF U.S. ADULTS WILL DEVELOP A MENTAL ILLNESS DURING THEIR LIFETIME.

People with severe mental illness are roughly twice as likely to commit violent acts in their lifetime.

THE FLOUNDERING MENTAL HEALTH SYSTEM

LESS THAN HALF OF AMERICANS WITH SEVERE MENTAL HEALTH ISSUES RECEIVE ADEQUATE TREATMENT.

$1.6 BILLION
in state mental health services have been cut since 2009.

These cuts led to significant reductions in hospital and community services for those with serious mental illness, including cutbacks to:
- HOUSING
- OUTPATIENT INTERVENTIONS
- MEDICATION
- CRISIS SERVICES

13%
of children ages 8 to 15 live with severe mental health disorders

but only

20%
of those children receive treatment

ONE-HALF OF ALL LIFETIME CASES OF MENTAL ILLNESS BEGIN BY AGE 14. THEY ARE LONG DELAYS — SOMETIMES DECADES — BETWEEN THE FIRST ONSET OF SYMPTOMS AND WHEN PEOPLE SEEK AND RECEIVE TREATMENT.

500,000:
TOTAL BEDS AVAILABLE IN AMERICAN MENTAL INSTITUTIONS AT PEAK LEVELS IN THE 1950s

43,000:
TOTAL BEDS CURRENTLY AVAILABLE

This shortage of inpatient psychiatric care makes finding appropriate care difficult, if not impossible.

IN 2011
24 STATES
SLASHED MENTAL HEALTH PROVIDER REIMBURSEMENT RATES FOR MEDICAID — THE MOST IMPORTANT SOURCE OF FUNDING OF PUBLIC MENTAL HEALTH SERVICES.

REAL
SOLUTIONS

"We must not only tackle gun control but we must also fix deficiencies in the U.S. mental health system, gaps that can leave many people with serious mental illnesses and family members without an easy way to get the treatment and support we know can help avert catastrophes."

Lynn R. Goldman, pediatrician and dean of the George Washington University School of Public Health and Health Services

New Assault Weapons Ban
Senator Dianne Feinstein has reintroduced the Assult Weapons Ban and large-capacity magazines.

Funding Mental Health Care
The recently passed Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act will expand Medicaid coverage to millions of uninsured Americans and includes requirements to cover mental health care.

Gun Violence Research
On January 16, President Obama signed an executive order directing the CDC and other federal agencies to research causes of gun violence and prevention strategies.

Destigmatizing Mental Illness
On February 4, Department of Health & Human Services Secretary Kathleen Sebelius announced initiatives to reduce stigma and expand access to mental health services, including improved parity laws and outreach to keep people from seeking care.

The Gutenberg machine - A European innovation

For the first time in history the Bible was printed.

A Gutenberg Press with these core components...

Moveable Type
Composing Stick
meant no more hand copying

LITHOGRAPHY
1796
Based on the immiscibility of oil and water, it leaves your fingers greasy.

A low-cost, fault-tolerant method

(illustrations of flasks and stones)

The press can be temperamental
Stones are heavy and fragile
Alois Senefelder

ROTARY PRESS
1842
the longest, fastest, biggest and newest printing machine to date

The first newspaper to use it was The Baltimore Sun.

OFFSET PRINTING
1875
Colours are separated and then 'offset' - the quality achievable with this process is outstanding.

Colours are separated into Cyan, Magenta, Yellow and Black

(icons showing rollers and colour separations)

The first way to 'compose' type for photographs and complex layouts

SCREEN PRINTING
1910
One of the cheapest printing techniques, it could transfer a design onto virtually anything.

Screen printing is fairly easy, here is what you need: (2)

A basic design
A silk screen mask
Waterbased ink
Squeegee

XEROGRAPHY
1938
copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy, copy...

INKJET PRINTING
1951
The computer can be in and do the first digital printers

Electricity (a power supply)
Ink Cartridges (for your colours)
Ink Tanks (becoming available towards the end of the century)
A computer (No need to be connected to a mainframe)

LASER PRINTING
1969
- light beam that 'impresses'

What's going on inside your printer?

Negative charge from the corona wire (or roller) attracts toner to the drum
A laser burns the image onto the drum and 'exposes' the areas to be printed
Toner is transferred to the paper
The drum and rollers then fuse the toner onto the paper using heat and pressure

3D PRINTING
1984
The design exits the boundaries of the canvas!

It uses coloured plastic filaments as ink. (2)

Many manufacturers now include 3D printers for prototyping and bespoke manufacture.
This puts ideas, objects and designs directly into the hands of makers.

FUTURE TECHNOLOGIES
2117

Measurement of the spicy heat (or piquance) of a chili pepper.
The number of Scoville heat units (SHU) indicates the amount of capsaicin* present.

Hotness        Scoville Units                    Examples
15,000,000–16,000,000   Pure capsaicin
8,600,000–9,100,000     Various capsaicinoids (e.g., homocapsaicin, homodihydrocapsaicin, nordihydrocapsaicin)
5,000,000–5,300,000     Law enforcement-grade pepper spray, FN 303 irritant ammunition
855,000–1,463,700       Naga Viper pepper, Infinity Chilli, Bhut Jolokia, Trinidad Scorpion Butch T pepper
350,000–800,000         Red Savina habanero
100,000–350,000         Habanero, Scotch bonnet, Datil pepper, Rocoto, Madame Jeanette, Peruvian White Habanero, Jamaican pepper
50,000–100,000          Byadgi chilli, Bird's eye chili, Malagueta pepper, Chiltepin pepper, Piri piri, Pequin pepper
30,000–50,000           Guntur chilli, Cayenne pepper, Aji pepper, Tabasco pepper, Cumari pepper (Capsicum Chinese), Chipotle
10,000–23,000           Serrano pepper, Peter pepper, Aleppo pepper
3,500–8,000             Espelette pepper, Jalapeño, Guajillo pepper, New Mexican varieties, abasco sauce
1,000–2,500             Anaheim pepper, Poblano pepper, Rocotillo pepper, Peppadew

*Capsaicin is a chemical compound that stimulates chemoreceptor nerve endings in the skin, especially the mucous membranes.

A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we’re on a path to a 4°C warmer world by the end of this century. The social, environmental, and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart investments, warming can still be held below 2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age
Since the last Ice Age to pre-industrial times (thousands of years ago):
-7°C

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750)
The global mean temperature rose by 4-5°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre-industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre-industrial Times (around 1750) → Today
Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.
The global mean temperature is 0.8°C higher.
Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Antarctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.
Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man-made change.

Today → Future
If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.
Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 meter.
By 2100:
4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

There would be larger impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.

Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.

Flooding and rising sea-level hotspots.

Sea level rise in the tropics is likely to be 15–20% higher than the global mean.

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
2°C warmer — Growth slows down
3°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forest fires could double
3°C warmer — Large sections could transform to savannah
4°C warmer — Large sections transform to low biomass woodlands

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual runoff decreases by 20–40% in the Danube, Mississippi, Amazon, and Murray-Darling Basins.
4°C warmer — All above changes likely to double

What can we do?
The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
- Make economic growth green and inclusive

- Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

question: How many types of Antimicrobials?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
Using
DISINFECTING
WIPES

at home & school

Types of Antimicrobials

Sanitizers
Reduce bacterial numbers on surfaces to a safe level.

Disinfectants
Kill bacteria, fungi, and some viruses on surfaces.

Antiseptics/Drugs
Antiseptics like hand sanitizers treat or prevent diseases on living things. They are not pesticides.

Microbes
Bacteria
Fungi
Viruses
Spores*
*Most wipes do not kill spores

What is dwell time?
-The contact time (seconds or minutes) the product needs to stay on a surface to kill microbes
-The surface should be visibly wet for the entire dwell time
-Time may change based on the microbe and purpose (sanitize vs. disinfect)

Where can they be used?
-Specific locations listed only on the label
-Use sites and dwell time vary by location and purpose

Household surfaces
Medical
Public Health

What are they?
- Antimicrobials are intended to kill or slow the growth of microbes
-Disinfectant wipes are disposable for hard surfaces like counters and tables
-Surface cleaners like wipes are not intended for use on skin
- Disinfectant wipes may LOOK similar to hand wipes. Read the label! Look for phrases like "disinfecting", "antibacterial", or "kills germs, viruses, and bacteria". When in doubt, call NPIC: 800-858-7378

Use in schools and daycares
-Only adults should use cleaning wipes – children should NOT use wipes
-Keep out of reach of children
-Follow the dwell time listed on the label
-Leave wipes in the original container
-Use the right product for each situation
-Parents: ask how children are involved in classroom cleaning

These wipes are NOT for:
Food contact
Produce
Hand wipes
Baby wipes

What is antimicrobial resistance?
If similar products are used repeatedly, they may not be as effective over time. This resistance can occur naturally, but overuse or misuse of antimicrobial products increases the chances. Follow the label and dwell time to reduce the risk of resistance.

How do I minimize risk?
-Always follow the label, including use sites and dwell times
-Never mix antimicrobial or cleaning products
-You can wear gloves if you prefer, always check the label first
-Wash hands after use
-Avoid touching wet surfaces after use
-Follow first aid instructions
-Open windows or use fans
-Store wipes out of reach of children
-Removing dirt and food from surfaces before helps wipes work
-Misuse or overuse of wipes can lead to antimicrobial resistance

Where can I get more information?
npic.orst.edu
npic@ace.orst.edu
800.858.7378

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

— The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.
— Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.
— The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness".
— In the 4th century C.E., Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

...and Christmas was born.

16
The average number of colleagues young employees are friends with on Facebook.

Who Should You Friend?

Should young professionals and students add employers?

Make this decision carefully - find out if other coworkers are friends with your manager first.

Pro
Personalized networking can be an asset when furthering your career.

Cons
Some employers may be uncomfortable to connect online, as they may have more rigid ideas of friendship on social networks.

Unprofessional activity online may impact your job if seen by your boss.

Also consider:
If you receive a friend request from a superior and want to accept, first clean up your profile and limit what he can see.

You may want to wait on friending a superior until they decide to friend you first.

If you receive a friend request from a superior and don't want to accept it, either ignore it or politely explain that you try to keep your personal and professional lives separate.

Remember to never post workplace complaints online.

More Options

Don't friend coworkers or associates you don't trust.

Should students add:

It's a great way to make friends and connect outside of class.

You can connect with others for homework help if you need it.

Classmates

Some students may not add you, but don't take this personally.

Faculty

Some faculty use social networks to expand on classroom discussions or to provide homework help.

Friending a teacher could lead to better learning and educational opportunities.

Some view teacher-student interactions on social media as inappropriate.

Should young professionals add:

Can help build inter-office relationships.

Connecting online may make balancing personal life and professional life difficult.

May lead to gossip and other inter-office problems.

Coworkers

Friending competitors can show that you support and appreciate their accomplishments.

Your competitor may learn company secrets from you.

Your current employer may think you want to leave.

Competitors

Control who can see your posts and information by customizing privacy settings.

Don't make it personal if someone decides not to friend you back.

MAKE SURE THE COLOR MODE IS RIGHT!

USE CMYK COLOR MODE

CMYK (Cyan-Magenta-Yellow-Black)
- Subtractive color model
- Refers to the 4 colors used in the printing process
- Smaller range of colors available than in RGB Mode
- Files must be saved in CMYK mode in order to be printed

RGB (Colors for Web)

RGB (Red-Green-Blue)
- Additive color model
- Red, Green and Blue colors are added together by light for a broader range of colors
- Any RGB files will automatically be converted to CMYK format when files are prepared for printing
- During RGB to CMYK file conversion there is color subtraction
- For consistency create design work in CMYK mode from start to finish

question: which touch screen technology is more costlier to make - resistive or infrared?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW DOES A TOUCHSCREEN PHONE WORK?

TECHNOLOGY

There are three types of technologies widely used in touch screen phones today: resistive, capacitive, and infrared.

RESISTIVE

PHONES
Samsung Messager Touch, Samsung Instinct, HTC Touch Diamond, LG Dare

HOW IT WORKS
Small dots separate multiple layers of material that are transmitting currents. When the top flexible layer is pushed into the lower layer, the electrical current changes and the location of impact is calculated.

COST TO MAKE
$

SCREEN MATERIAL
A layer of flexible material (usually polyester film) over a layer of glass.

TOOL FOR INPUT
Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY
Poor visibility

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?
No

DURABILITY
Fair Durability. Screens are vulnerable to scratches and other minor damage. The screen will wear over time and need replacing.

CAPACITIVE

PHONES
Huawei Ascend, Sanyo Zio, Apple's iPhone, HTC Hero, DROID Eris, Palm Pre, Blackberry Storm

HOW IT WORKS
The current is broadcast from the corners of the screen. When a finger touches the screen, it changes the current, and that location is calculated.

COST TO MAKE
$$

SCREEN MATERIAL
Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT
Bare fingers only

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY
Good visibility

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?
Yes

DURABILITY
Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

INFRARED (HEAT-SENSITIVE AND OPTICAL)

PHONES
Samsung U600 (heat), Neonode N2 (optical)

HOW IT WORKS
HEAT-SENSITIVE
Heat-sensitive must be touched with a warm object to register point of impact.

OPTICAL
Optical uses sensors to shoot a grid of invisible beams just above the screen. Point of input is calculated based upon the point where the x-y axis is interrupted.

COST TO MAKE
$$$

SCREEN MATERIAL
Glass

TOOL FOR INPUT
Optical: Bare fingers, gloved fingers, or a stylus
Heat-Sensitive: Warm, bare fingers

OUTDOOR VISIBILITY
Good visibility, but strong ambient light impacts productivity and precision.

MULTI-TOUCH CAPABILITIES?
Yes

DURABILITY
Good Durability. Glass shatters upon major impact only.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Touchscreen

EMPLOYEE SOCIAL MEDIA USAGE AT WORK
Statistics

How much of work time taken up per person in an average working day?

none 32.8%
1-15 mins 18.7%
15-30 mins 17.2%
30-60 mins 12.3%
60-120 mins 9.6%
+120 mins 9.4%

This equates to a cost per employee per year in wasted wages of:

None 32.8% — none
1-15 mins 18.7% — up to £1,019.50
15-30 mins 17.2% — up to £2,039
30-60 mins 12.3% — up to £4,078
60-120 mins 9.6% — up to £8,156
+120 mins 9.4% — over £8,156

When employees use social media at work

40% intermittently throughout the day
11.6% during toilet breaks
4.5% other
41.4% during lunch
22.3% at the end of the day
6.4% when first arriving at the office

Why employees use social media at work

34% use social media while at work to take a mental break from their job
27% to connect with friends and family while at work
24% to make or support professional connections
17% to build or strengthen personal relationships with coworkers
20% to get information that helps them solve problems at work
17% to learn about someone they work with
12% to ask work-related questions of people outside their organisation
12% to ask such questions of people inside their organisation

Who is using it most:

25 mins women
39 mins men

Employers with and without social media policies

39% with
61% without

Impact of having a social media policy

Workers whose companies have an at-work social media policy say they use social media while on the job to:

30% take a break from work, compared with 40% of workers whose employers do not have such policies.
20% stay connected to family and friends while on the job, compared with 35% of workers whose social media use is not regulated at work.
Only 16% get information that’s helpful to their job, compared with 25% of those whose workplaces have no such regulations.

How employees view their use of social media

56% believe social media ultimately helps their job performance
22% believe that it mostly hurts
16% feel that it doesn’t have much impact either way
6% see both the benefits and the drawbacks

The Cost of Employee Social Media

Averaged this is a cost of £2,592.60 per employee per year.

Multiplied by 30,645,000 employees in the UK, this is an annual cost to employers of
£79,450,227,000 i.e. nearly £80 billion.

Factoring in lost productivity, this would cost even more.
8.13% of the working week is being lost on average.
8.13% of GDP is £185,884,320,000 i.e. nearly £186 billion in GDP lost every year to the UK.

30,645,000 people employed in the UK in 2016.
£2,286,400,000,000 GDP for UK in 2015.

Source/notes:
• The cost per employee per year is based on a 2012 government report (NICE’s Workplace Health report - Local government briefing). This is a conservative estimate based on median salary scales. Wages used are an average of public and private sector pay.
• Multiplied by the number of people employed in the UK (30,645,000) to give an annual cost to employers.
• Factoring lost productivity against UK GDP gives an estimated GDP lost per year.

http://www.ons.gov.uk
http://www.nice.org.uk

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
A 4°C WARMER WORLD
We Can and Must Avoid It

Climate change is happening — now. Turn Down the Heat, a report on the latest climate science prepared for the World Bank, notes we're on a path to a 4°C Celsius warmer world by the end of this century. The social, environmental, and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. This is not inevitable, however — with sustained policy action and climate-smart solutions, warming can still be held below +2°C.

Human impact on the climate system is unequivocal.

Ice Age → Pre‑industrial Times (around 1750)

Since the last Ice Age to pre‑industrial times (thousands of years ago):
Sea levels have risen 15–120 cm.

The global mean temperature rose by 4–5°C.

The same warming is taking place from pre‑industrial times to 2100 (only about 300 years).

Pre‑industrial Times (around 1750) → Today

Sea levels have risen 15–20 cm.

The global mean temperature is ~0.8°C higher.

Ice sheets in both Greenland and the Arctic are losing mass at an accelerating rate.

Since 2002, 13 extreme weather events like floods, heatwaves, and hurricanes have been linked to man‑made change.

Today → Future

If present emission trends continue, the world is on a path to a 4°C warmer world.

Likely sea level rise: 0.5–1 meter

By 2100:
4° warmer

What could a 4°C warmer world look like?
Warming is likely to have more severe impacts on the tropics.

There would be longer impacts on agriculture and ecosystems.

Drought and aridity would likely increase in tropical developing countries.

Flooding and rising sea‑level hotspots

Sea level rise is likely to be 15–20% higher in the tropics than the global mean

Corals Driven to Extinction
Current
0.8°C warmer — Threatened with increased bleaching
2°C warmer — Growth slows down
3°C warmer — Begin to dissolve
4°C warmer — Extinction possible

Amazon Rainforest Dieback
Current
0.8°C warmer — Home to rich biodiversity
2°C warmer — Forests could die back
3°C warmer — Fires could double
4°C warmer — Large sections transition into low biomass woodlands

Water Resources Threatened
2°C warmer — Mean annual runoff decreases by 10–30% in the Mediterranean, Middle East, parts of southern Africa, Central America and the Caribbean, and parts of Australia*
4°C warmer — Mean annual runoff decreases by 20–40% in the Mediterranean, Middle East, parts of southern Africa, Central America and the Caribbean, and parts of Australia*
All above changes likely to double

What can we do?

The steps we take today can help countries transition to low carbon and climate resilient growth paths for the future.

WE MUST:
Make economic growth green and inclusive
Scale up affordable technology solutions
Invest in climate-smart infrastructure

Increasing finances to fight climate change

World Bank lending in 2012:
$7.1B
Mitigation

$4.6B
Adaptation

Making development climate‑smart

Agriculture
Kenya
First soil carbon project
Higher yields, greater resilience, and lower emissions across 450,000 hectares

Transport
Nigeria
Green urban transport in Lagos
Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) system used by ~200,000 commuters/day

Energy
Bangladesh
Solar panels for off‑grid homes
1.4 million low‑income rural homes have electricity

Water
China
Integrating water resource management with pollution control
20 million+ people benefit

Policy
India
Development Policy Loan
Launching transformative actions in energy, water, industry, and tourism in Himachal Pradesh

Risk Analysis
Vietnam
Decision‑making under uncertainty
Helping policymakers make the right decisions on infrastructure in Ho Chi Minh

To learn more, go to climatechange.worldbank.org
Visit the World Bank on Facebook, and follow us on twitter @wbclimatechange

Source: Turn Down the Heat: Why a 4°C warmer world must be avoided

Social Media Platform
Cheat Sheet

Pinterest

Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.

Best for:
Link clicks

Resource and expertise level needed:
High

Good for these business types:
Restaurants
Interior design
Wedding venues

Twitter

Key differentiating trait:
Fast-moving, real-time, news-driven platform. Strong customer service element, be prepared to engage and respond to customers here.

Best for:
Link clicks, engagement, and foot traffic

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Retail
Nonprofits
Newspapers & media outlets

LinkedIn

Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.

Best for:
Link clicks, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium

Good for these business types:
Luxury goods & services
Software providers
B2B Companies

Instagram

Key differentiating trait:
There are very few ways to link from Instagram, so this is a brand-building platform. You need to have lots of high-quality images and videos to support this platform.

Best for:
Foot traffic, word-of-mouth awareness, engagement

Resource and expertise level needed:
Medium to high

Good for these business types:
Bakeries & Coffee Shops
Travel agencies
Art museums

Facebook

Key differentiating trait:
This is the platform, but it doesn’t mean you need to be there. With the recent challenges the platform has faced, some users are choosing to spend their social media time elsewhere (like Instagram, which is also a Facebook platform). That being said, there’s still no better place to find and engage customers, donors, or others.

Best for:
Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations

Resource and expertise level needed:
Low

Good for these business types:
Local Brick & Mortar Stores
Nonprofits
B2B Companies

Constant Contact

Traditional TV and radio ads are being edged out by a younger, fresher digital option: influencer marketing.

Like
Influencer marketing pairs social media influencers, who have large trusted audiences on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram or Pinterest, with businesses looking to promote their products to new and existing customers through the influencer's channels.

This effective advertising option is taking off. Research shows 54% of companies have a stand-alone budget for sponsored social for their brand.

Social media icons with a great following and credibility within a certain niche. They could be:

YouTube Users
Bloggers
Celebrities
Industry Experts
Activists
Enthusiasts

Collaborate with businesses to create campaigns that increase brand awareness and drive sales by promoting a brand or product to their social audience.

CREATE
Value-added content about a product or service to share with their blog readers and/or social channels

POST
Product reviews, contests, giveaways, coupons, personal stories about products or services, videos, and more

INFLUENCE
Share content across multiple channels to increase awareness of the brand, product, or service

HOW INFLUENCERS MAKE MONEY
Businesses compensate influencers monetarily and/or with free samples of a product.

HIGH ENGAGEMENT

SOCIAL SPONSORSHIP

BUILDING LOW-CARBON CITIES
Where development challenges and solutions meet

CHALLENGE:
• Today, just over 50% of the global population is urban. That's growing.

6.2 BILLION PEOPLE
will be living in cities by 2050
That will be 70% of the world's population

70%
70%

Cities already account for
2/3 of the world's overall energy consumption
and 70% of global greenhouse emissions

Growing urbanization means that more and more people and assets in cities will be exposed to extreme events:

With sea level rise and land subsidence,
flood damage in large coastal cities could reach
$1 TRILLION A YEAR by 2050

The number of people exposed to storms and earthquakes in large cities will more than double
to 1.5 BILLION PEOPLE by 2050

OPPORTUNITY:
Cities offer a unique opportunity to tackle climate change

If the world's cities embark on a low-carbon development path, GLOBAL GREENHOUSE GAS EMISSIONS could DECREASE by
30% or (10 GIGATONS)

EQUIVALENT TO 2X the carbon footprint of the ENTIRE EUROPEAN UNION

SOLUTION:
Toward livable, low-carbon and resilient cities

PLANNING:
Helping cities plan for low-carbon development

1 DEVELOP GREENHOUSE GAS INVENTORIES
GHG inventories provide a snapshot of a city's emissions profile and the percentage of overall emissions from different sectors.

"If you can't measure it, you can't manage it"
MAYOR BLOOMBERG
(C40 Chair)

Only about 25% of the 150 LARGEST CITIES
(in both developing and developed countries) have even the basic analytics needed for low-carbon planning, such as greenhouse gas inventories.

2 PROVIDE TOOLS TO EVALUATE THE EMISSION REDUCTION POTENTIAL
of investments and define climate action plans.
(Climate action plans help a city define its mitigation potential, including the mix of policies and interventions needed to achieve emissions reduction targets.)

City Type X Climate Action Plan =
EMISSIONS REDUCTION POTENTIAL

3 STANDARDIZE "CLIMATE-SMART" CAPITAL INVESTMENT PLANNING
at the city level

(Climate-smart capital investment plans determine the investments needed to carry out the climate action plans.)

FINANCING:
Helping cities get finance flowing so they can set themselves on a low-carbon and resilient development path

OVER $1 TRILLION A YEAR
is needed to finance the infrastructure gap in LOW- and MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

70% of that trillion would be absorbed by cities

International aid for development is currently
$125 BILLION
and is only a small part of the solution

Options to help cities access the financing they need:

1 ENHANCE CREDITWORTHINESS TO ENABLE DIRECT ACCESS TO CAPITAL MARKETS
by working with city financial officers to review their revenue and debt

HOW IT
WORKS

Small dots separate multiple layers of material that are transmitting currents. When the top flexible layer is pushed into the lower layer, the electrical current changes and the location of impact is calculated.

question: What are the points to be kept in mind while reading?
prediction_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini: Draw connections to other parts of the material
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
7
SCIENCE-BACKED WAYS
READING
Makes You Healthy

1
Increases longevity by 23%

Study:
A 2016 study observed the reading habits of 3653 people. Study participants were classified into those who:
• Read books for 3.5 hours or more per week
• Read books for up to 3.5 hours a week
• Did not read at all

The result:
When followed up over 12 years, it was found that those who:
• Read books for more than 3.5 hours a week were 23% less likely to die when compared to those who did not read books.
• Read books for up to 3.5 hours a week were 17% less likely to die when compared to those who did not read books.

Why do book readers live longer?
Reading books provides a higher survival advantage than reading newspapers or magazines because book readers undergo "deep reading".

This means readers:
• Draw connections to other parts of the material
• Find applications to the outside world
• Ask questions about the content

Deep reading aids survival because:
1 Engaging the brain throughout your life in mentally stimulating activities such as deep reading reduces cognitive decline.
2 Deep reading promotes:
Empathy
Social perception
Emotional intelligence
3 These lead to better relationships and a good social network.

A good social network has been shown to be associated with a higher survival rate.
• Friends can provide support and a source of comfort, shielding from the harmful impacts of stress.
• Friends can also bring purpose and meaning into people’s lives.

2
Reduces stress

Reading reduces stress levels by 68%, more than any other activity.

READING 68%
LISTENING TO MUSIC 61%
DRINKING TEA OR COFFEE 54%
WALKING 42%

Just 6 minutes of reading:
• Slows your heart rate
• Eases muscle tension
• Alters your state of mind

3
Promotes relaxation and sleep

Reading can help you sleep if you make it a part of your bedtime ritual.

Why?
• It helps relax your body and mind
• It provides a signal that it is almost time to go to bed

Make sure it's a print book.
It's best to be reading a print book in the evening if you are about to go to sleep as evening use of e-readers and smartphones can disrupt your circadian rhythm and disturb sleep.

4
Staves off Alzheimer's disease, dementia and mental decline

• Readers are 2.5 times less likely to develop Alzheimer's disease.
• Frequent brain exercises, like reading, decreases mental decline in the elderly by 32%.

Why?
• Reading is mentally stimulating and helps keep the brain active.
• When you read you have to remember a lot of things, like:
Characters
Main plot
Sub-plots
• All the developments throughout a story are a new memory in your brain and therefore new neural pathways are created and existing ones strengthened.
• The brain operates very much like a muscle — you have to exercise it to keep it healthy and strong.

5
Helps with depression

Reading can be an important non-medical intervention for people with depression.

Reading group trials, conducted at a mental health day-hospital and at a GP surgery centre, for people diagnosed with depression, have been shown to reduce symptoms of depression in participants.

The participants in the reading group reported feeling more:
• Confident
• Talkative
• Willing to listen
• Willing to interact with the other group participants

6
Boosts happiness

Life experiences make you happier than material things.

A recent study showed that experiential products designed to create or enhance life experiences, like books, are total happiness boosters.

Other experiential products include:
SPORTING GOODS
VIDEO GAMES
MUSICAL INSTRUMENTS

Reading can also help fill up your fulfillment tank.
• A study found that older adults who continue to read and learn are much more satisfied with their lives.
• Another study has shown that those who read books reported better social connections and more happiness with their life.

7
Increases social connection

Reading develops theory of mind, which leads to better social connections.

Definition: Theory of Mind is the ability to ascribe mental states, such as feelings, beliefs, thoughts and goals, to yourself and to others. It is the capacity to understand that other people's feelings and beliefs may be different from your own.

Social connections increase life expectancy
• A meta-analysis done across 148 studies showed that people who have a good social network tended to live longer.
• The absence of social connections is just as bad as smoking up to 15 cigarettes a day.
• Loneliness and social isolation have been shown to be associated with early death.

- The gauntlet worn by Thanos in the trailer is an incomplete/false gauntlet. The real Infinity Gauntlet is a more refined artifact capable of holding all six stones permanently. The prop shown appears to be a crude mounting device for the stones on Thanos' hand rather than the finished gauntlet seen in comics.

Universes & Hollywood

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
- The MCU films are produced by Marvel Studios and distributed by Walt Disney Studios Motion Pictures.
- Marvel Studios operates under a distinct continuity that connects all movies in the MCU timeline.
- Characters include Iron Man, Captain America, Thor, Hulk, Black Widow, Hawkeye, Guardians of the Galaxy, Doctor Strange, Ant-Man, Spider-Man (co-produced with Sony), Black Panther, and others.

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
- DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures produce films based on characters from DC Comics.
- Includes Man of Steel, Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice, Suicide Squad, Wonder Woman, Justice League.
- Separate continuity from Marvel's films.

20TH CENTURY FOX
- Fox owns film rights to the X-Men and Fantastic Four characters.
- Includes the X-Men series, Deadpool, Fantastic Four films.
- Not part of the MCU continuity (unless rights are reclaimed or deals made).

SONY PICTURES
- Sony owns the film rights to Spider-Man characters.
- Sony and Marvel Studios reached an agreement to share Spider-Man for the MCU, allowing Tom Holland to appear in MCU films like Captain America: Civil War and Spider-Man: Homecoming while Sony retains distribution rights to solo Spider-Man films.

- Loki uses the Tesseract in The Avengers.
- The Mind Stone is housed in Loki's scepter and later becomes part of Vision.
- The Reality Stone appears as the Aether in Thor: The Dark World.
- The Power Stone was contained in the Orb seen in Guardians of the Galaxy.
- The Time Stone is introduced in Doctor Strange as the Eye of Agamotto.
- The Soul Stone's nature and location are revealed in later events; requires a price.

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...
a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape
tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP
your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your left elbow next to your paper! If you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT
tape two pencils together with sticky tape
hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen
keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN
never dip your pen directly into the ink pot
use your brush to gather a horizontal stripe of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib
you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...
put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!
make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch
cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a faint line between the two dots. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form a guide for measuring the size and height of your calligraphy letters.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib heights. Using your nib ladder draw the x-height across your paper to form your main guideline.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure about 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw your ascender and descender lines.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It sits above the x-height and below the ascender line.

WARNING
If you're right-handed your nib holder will point to the left of you when you write; if you're left-handed your nib holder will point to the right. Always protect your work surface and clothing - ink stains are hard to remove. We recommend using a chisel-tip marker for practice; the pen will give better control once you're more confident.

CELEBRATE THE
CHINESE
NEW YEAR
WITH
6 LUCKY FOODS

春節
CHINESE

101

THE CHINESE NEW YEAR IS ALSO KNOWN AS THE
SPRING FESTIVAL & LUNAR NEW YEAR.

The date changes every year because it is based on the lunar-solar Chinese calendar.     The festival happens in winter, when people look forward to the renewal of spring.

THE CHINESE NEW YEAR,
春節, CHUN JIE or “CHUN-JYEAH”
has been observed for more than 3,000 years.

THE
YEAR OF THE PIG
OFFICIALLY BEGINS
FEBRUARY 5,
2019.

IT WILL
GIVE WAY TO THE
YEAR OF THE RAT
ON
JANUARY 25,
2020

People observing Chinese New Year often celebrate with special decorations, firecrackers and fireworks, and red envelopes filled with money.

CHINESE NEW YEAR DECORATIONS
are often red because red is considered lucky in the Chinese culture.

CHINESE NEW YEAR FOODS
are often deemed lucky based on symbolic meanings and/or if the name of a food sounds similar to other Chinese words for luck or wealth.

6 LUCKY
CHINESE NEW YEAR
FOODS

1
CITRUS FRUIT
- In Chinese the word for pomelo sounds like the word for to have.
- Tangerines, mandarins, and kumquats are said to evoke gold, so having them can bring wealth and increase prosperity.
- The word for orange or tangerine sounds similar to the Chinese words for success.
- Another way to write tangerine is comprised of the Chinese character for luck.

2
DUMPLINGS
- Chinese dumplings symbolize great wealth.
- Depending on how they are prepared, dumplings can be made to resemble silver ingots or gold bars.

3
SPRING ROLLS OR EGG ROLLS
- Similar to dumplings, egg rolls and spring rolls may resemble gold bars.
- Chinese New Year spring rolls symbolize wealth.

4
LONGEVITY NOODLES
- The length of the noodles represents long life.
- Cutting the noodles into small pieces is frowned upon, as it means the person's life is to be cut short.

5
RICE CAKE
- In Chinese legend, rice cakes would be prepared and served to a Chinese god of the kitchen, in the hopes that when the god returned to heaven before the new year, the god would speak well of the family.
- The Chinese words for these cakes sound like the words for growing or getting higher every year, so they symbolize a prosperous year ahead.
- The sweetness of the cakes also represents a sweet life.

6
WHOLE FISH
- Serving whole fish symbolizes prosperity.
- The Chinese word for fish sounds like the Chinese word for surplus. Traditionally, Chinese people believed in having a surplus of money or goods at the end of the year because having something leftover meant they could make more in the year to come.

CHINESE
NEW YEAR
DUMPLING LORE

Flat dumplings symbolize poverty, so New Year dumplings are often shaped with many pleats.

If you eat a dumpling that contains a white thread, you'll have bad luck.  (illustration)

A dumpling with a coin inside means you'll get rich.

When serving, dumplings should be arranged in lines, not circles, because a circle suggests that you won't make progress in life and will keep going in circles.

To pass on wishes of amassing wealth, when eating dumplings it's traditional to include a coin in one dumpling and the person who finds it will have good fortune.

"ZHAO CAI JIN BAO"
JAOW TSEYE JIN BAOWW
招财进宝
meaning "Bring in wealth and treasure"

VISUAL HISTORY OF
Christmas Trees

The first Christmas tree was the centerpiece of holiday festivities in fifteenth century Latvia. Young men and women danced and sang around the tree before setting it on fire the last night of festivities. From those early traditions, to the first American tree in 1816, and into the present Christmas season, Christmas trees have been the focal point of holiday cheer. This timeline takes a look at some of the significant moments in the modern history of this Christmas icon.

Landmark trees
Decoration inventions
Christmas culture
Tree industry

1600
First decorated tree

1883
First artificial tree
Sears, Roebuck & Company began offering the first artificial Christmas trees -- 33 limbs for $.50 and 55 limbs for $1.00.

“It’s not good to cut down trees for mere decoration,” says President Teddy Roosevelt in 1901, launching a century long debate on real vs. artificial trees.

1910
1910s Glass candleholders & tinsel trees

Small electric lights are already invented, but are too expensive and dangerous for trees.

Overharvesting leads to evergreen shortages, leading to

1920s Tree farms
The industry grows rapidly. By 2002, over 40 million trees are sold in the U.S. every Christmas.

1923 National Christmas Tree
The same large evergreen has been decorated every year since 1923. In 2011, the tree fell in a windstorm and a new one was planted.

1930
Brush company manufactures first bristle trees

1920s Feathered trees
Imported from Germany, feather trees become popular in U.S. and range in size from 2 inches to 6 foot.

1933 Rockefeller Center
The tallest Christmas tree ever at Rockefeller was a 30 foot spruce in 1999.

1940s Tree flocking
The West Coast reinvents Christmas with flocking kits that can be applied with vacuum cleaners.

1946 Bubble lights

1955 Disneyland Main Street Tree
Sixty foot tall live tree until 2008, when they switched to an artificial one for environmental reasons.

1958
Aluminum trees are first non-green artificial trees.

1957 How The Grinch Stole Christmas
Two classics that remind everyone it's the spirit of giving, not a tree, that makes Christmas.

1965 A Charlie Brown Christmas
Charlie's negative portrayal of artificial trees causes dip in sales.

1966 White House Blue Room Tree
National Christmas Tree Association begins presenting the White House with a tree from the annual Grand Champion award-winning growers.

1970-80s
Plastic trees lead to explosion of artificial trees as green alternative.

In 1992, 46% of homes used an artificial tree. The number jumped to 58% in 2004.

1980 Largest tree lit in Gubbio, Italy
8.5 kilometers of cable outlines a 650 meter tall tree.

1989 A Christmas Vacation
The Griswold Family's quest for the perfect tree is full of hilarious holiday disasters.

1990s Fiber-optic trees

2000s War on Christmas
Politicians debate whether it’s more politically correct to refer to Christmas trees as ‘holiday’ trees.

Meanwhile, sales continue to boom. Over 17.4 million artificial trees were sold in 2007.

New consumer technologies drive a new era of Christmas tree decorations.

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your left wrist over the top of your paper - if you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape
hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen
keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot
use your brush to paint a horizontal stripe of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib
you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!
make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch
cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

adgh

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line along the bottom of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form a guide for measuring the size and height of your calligraphy letters. Put your pen parallel to your ruler and draw a square shape the size of your pen nib on the paper. From the top of your first square, draw a second square the same size and height as your first. Continue in this way until you have formed a diagonal line of 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib heights tall. Using your nib ladder, measure 4-5 nib heights up from the baseline and draw a horizontal line. This is your x-height line - keep your pen upright with your pencil.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below and draw two more horizontal lines to mark the ascender and descender heights. These lines indicate the top and bottom limits for letters.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It is usually around the ascender height. Measure using your nib ladder and draw a horizontal cap-height line.

WARNING
If you're designing your nib ladder with gouache, allow it to dry before using your pen. We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker to practice before using your nib pen until your grid gets comfortable.

question: Which year was Facebook founded?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
friendster
THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platforms every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING

Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?".

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT

ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect Universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if a transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately, the letters 'LO' weren't the complete 'LOGIN' that they had planned to send over.

START OF SOCIAL SITES

From 1997 to 2001, Six Degrees had a peak of about 1 million members who were able to create profiles and "friend" each other.

1997

CREATIVITY AND STATUS BLOGS

LiveJournal was a platform where users could blog as a form of keeping friends updated with their lives, a format later used by Facebook for their status.

1999

A CHALLENGER EMERGES

Friendster, one of the first social media sites, is created and launched by Peter Chin, Jonathan Abrams, and Owen Lee. Within the next year they had 3 million users. Google, widely considered one of the biggest blunders of Silicon Valley.

2002

LINK UP WITH BUSINESSES

LinkedIn gave users a place to post their resume and private message people with a business-orientated mindset. It's a very popular way for businesses to recruit new employees and for users to search for jobs. By 2004 the number of users on the site would reach 1 million, 3 years later they would break 500 million users.

2003

GMAIL RECEIVED

Google creates its own email service, Gmail, in January. In the UK Gmail was branded as Google Mail, due to a trademark dispute, until 2009 when they changed the branding back after the dispute was over.

2004

FACEBOOK BEGINS

In February 2004, Facebook was launched by Mark Zuckerberg, along with his Harvard roommates. The site was initially limited to Harvard students but in the following months and years, its membership would be worldwide, reaching the number of users to around 2.2 billion in 14 years.

2004

BIRTH OF YOUTUBE

YouTube a platform on which users can upload and share videos, was made public in February 2005. The first video to hit one million views was "Me at the Zoo" and within this year, thanks to a mix of features, the site exploded. Facebook is now available in the UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia and New Zealand on October 1st.

2005

MYSPACE IS BOOMING

Myspace became the most popular social network in the US during 2006! That's just 3 years after it was founded.

Twitter is launched publically on July 15th

2006

750 TWEETS PER SECOND!

65 million tweets a day are being sent out on Twitter, that's around 750 tweets every second.

2010

LAUNCH OF SNAPCHAT

Snapchat, an image messaging app, is launched in September after being tested as an iOS only app known as Picaboo in July. One of the founding members is pushed out before Snapchat is released.

2011

SHORT VIDEOS, MEGA VIEWS

Vine, a video hosting service specialising in short videos, goes public in June. Twitter acquires Vine for $30 million in the October of that year.

2013

UK'S INSTA ADS

Instagram brings sponsored Ads to target the UK in September following their inclusion on the app in America.

2014

LIFE OF A STARMAN

NASA's #YearInSpace started, giving Twitter followers updates on Scott Kelly an astronaut who lived in space for a year!

2015

VR AND FAKE NEWS

Virtual Reality, live videos, and 360-degree videos required all video content on social media sites like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram.
There was a crackdown on fake news throughout social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter.

2018

After all of this, we now have a beautiful array of social media channels to pick and choose from; Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Snapchat and more. We wonder what next advancement social media creators will think up. What do you think will happen in the future world of social media?

question: In which year first national bank established?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
A HISTORY OF
AMERICAN PAPER MONEY

FROM COLONIAL BILLS TO SILVER AND GOLD CERTIFICATES TO EVEN A $100,000 NOTE, AMERICAN PAPER MONEY HAS UNDERGONE MANY CHANGES THROUGHOUT ITS HISTORY.

1781
FIRST NATIONAL BANK
The Congress charters the Bank of North America as the first bank chartered by the United States.

1775
CONTINENTAL CONGRESS
Paper money is issued by the Continental Congress to finance the Revolutionary War.

COLONIAL BILLS
The Massachusetts Bay Colony issues the first paper currency to pay for soldiers and supplies. Other colonies follow.

1785
THE U.S. DOLLAR
The dollar becomes the official monetary unit of the United States.

1791
FIRST U.S. CENTRAL BANK
Congress charters the Bank of the United States. The First Bank operates for 20 years as a central bank for the U.S. Treasury.

1792
FEDERAL MINT SYSTEM
The Coinage Act establishes the U.S. Mint and creates the U.S. dollar coin.

1816
SECOND U.S. CENTRAL BANK
Congress charters the Second Bank of the United States as a central bank for the newly formed country.

1836
FREE BANKING ERA
States chartered banks begin issuing their own banknotes. Many banks failed and private banknotes were often of dubious value.

1861
CIVIL WAR "GREENBACKS"
In an effort to finance the Civil War, the government issues demand notes and later legal tender notes, the first to use green ink on the back.

1862
FIRST $2, $5, $10 AND $100 BILLS ARE ISSUED
Legal Tender Notes are U.S. Notes. Later replaced. Notes were made from 1862 to 1971.

1865
ESTABLISHMENT OF U.S. SECRET SERVICE
The U.S. Secret Service was created as part of the Treasury Department to investigate counterfeiting and to protect the nation's currency.

1877
BUREAU OF ENGRAVING AND PRINTING
The Treasury Department's Bureau of Engraving and Printing takes over production and printing.

1878
FIRST SILVER CERTIFICATES
Silver certificates start being issued in exchange for other dollars.

1882
FIRST GOLD CERTIFICATES
Gold certificates were authorized in 1882 for use by government and banks. Used from 1882 to 1933.

1913
FEDERAL RESERVE ACT OF 1913
The Federal Reserve System is created to act as the central banking system of the United States. Federal Reserve Notes are created.

1914
LARGE SIZE FEDERAL RESERVE NOTES
$1, $2, $5, $10, $20, $50, $100, $500 and $1,000 were issued in large-size notes beginning in 1914.

1918
LARGE SIZE HIGH DENOMINATIONS FEDERAL RESERVE NOTES
$500, $1,000, $5,000, $10,000 and $100,000 were printed for use in transactions between banks. These were not circulated among the general public.

1929
STANDARDIZED DESIGN
To lower printing costs and standardize currency, notes were redesigned; small-size notes with standardized dimensions and common portrait locations were introduced.

1934
$100,000 GOLD CERTIFICATES
The highest denomination note ever issued, these were used only for transactions between Federal Reserve Banks and were not circulated to the public.

1957
"IN GOD WE TRUST"
$1 Silver certificates were the first notes to have the motto "In God We Trust".

1990
NEW COUNTERFEIT DETERRENT METHODS
Micro-printing and security threads are developed to deter counterfeiters.

1996-2000
REDESIGN OF PAPER MONEY
Multiple redesigned denominations introduce new portraits, watermark features and enhanced security threads. New smaller security features are introduced.

2003-2006
UPDATED SECURITY FEATURES
Improved security features and subtle background colors are introduced on the $5, $10, $20 and $50 notes. These are the first notes to use multiple colors.

2007
NEW $5 BILL WITH AN ALL DIGITAL DESIGN
This was the first bill to feature added security devices and enhanced portrait imaging.

2010
NEW $100 BILL DESIGN
The new $100 bill is released featuring advanced security features including a blue 3D security ribbon and color-shifting bell in the inkwell, improving the bill's resistance to counterfeiting.

References

question: How many steps are there in the cycle of domestic abuse?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
BREAK THE CYCLE:
STEPS TO PROTECT YOUR FAMILY AGAINST VIOLENCE

THE CYCLE OF DOMESTIC ABUSE

Developed in the 1970s, The Cycle of Abuse by Lenore Walker explores the stages of an abusive relationship:

TENSION

Tension lays the foundation for domestic violence. The abuser - a husband, father or boyfriend - begins to exhibit passive-aggressive behaviour toward his victim - a wife, mother or girlfriend. The relationship becomes strained and tension builds as the behaviour worsens. Victims here notoriously try to change their behaviour to soften the intensity of their partner's abuse.

INCIDENT

But as it nearly always turns out - no level of obedience on the part of the victim will stop their aggressor's rage. Passive-aggressive behaviour eventually erupts in an overt or covert act of abuse - from physical abuse; hitting, kicking, shoving or throwing objects, to full-on control behaviours; such as emotional abuse, neglect or intimidation.

RECONCILIATION

After the act of abuse is over, the victim is left shaken, questioning how she landed in this situation. At this point, the abuser starts to backtrack on his actions - and is quick to repent, resolve, and reassure his bruised victim that he'll change - that this will never happen again. He tries to provide a blanket solution to the incident - buying presents or threatening self-harm if she leaves.

CALM

The calm stage - much like the calm before the storm - is thought to extend the reconciliation period as relative peace between the couple now ensues. The victim is now in the thick of a very destructive situation - blindsided to their partner's pleas that he has changed. But the calm only lasts so long as the storm begins to brew.

THE CYCLE ENDS WITH YOU

The road to end domestic violence is not impossible. Follow these practical steps to reverse the damage before it takes hold:

Tell someone about your experience
Reach out for help and don't go it alone - speak to family or friends you trust about your situation.

Safely challenge the behaviour
If you witness actual physical violence against a woman, call the police on 131 444 or 000 in the case of emergency.

Call the NSW Domestic Violence Line
For direct advice or help, you can contact a trained and qualified domestic violence advisor on 1800 656 463.

Consider the Aurora App
If you're experiencing domestic violence right now, or are worried you might be entering the cycle, download the Aurora App (free) for domestic violence risk support and services.

Lead by example and hold yourself accountable to the actions of others
Swear never to commit, excuse, or remain silent about domestic violence. Check out The White Ribbon Campaign today.

1996 CBR launches the 1st program to offer free banking services to families with medical need.

Universes & Hollywood

Marvel Cinematic Universe
- The idea of a shared universe was initially introduced in the 1960s comics. In the MCU, the films are explicitly linked and characters cross over from film to film, with an overall throughline driven by the Infinity Stones and Thanos' quest to collect them all.
- Marvel Studios produced the films above as part of Phases One, Two and Three.
- The MCU is known for mid-credits and post-credits scenes that set up future movies.

DC Extended Universe
- DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures created the DC Extended Universe (DCEU). The DCEU attempts to create a similar shared universe to the MCU with characters like Superman, Batman, Wonder Woman, Aquaman and the Flash appearing across titles, although the tone and approach differ.

20th CENTURY FOX
- Although they announced plans to bring the X-Men into a shared universe, these plans were carried out in their own continuity, separate from DC and the MCU.

SONY PICTURES
- Sony controls the film rights to Spider-Man and some associated characters. A deal with Marvel allows Spider-Man to appear in the MCU (e.g., Captain America: Civil War; Spider-Man: Homecoming) while Sony still produces standalone Spider-Man films.

The "false" gauntlet

- In Avengers: Age of Ultron, Tony Stark and Bruce Banner create a prototype gauntlet for Thor to wield that looks similar to the Infinity Gauntlet but is not the true gauntlet Thanos uses.
- This gauntlet was created as a prop and cosmetic measure for battle, not as the actual artifact capable of channeling all six Infinity Stones.
- The real Infinity Gauntlet that Thanos obtains is sturdier, properly forged to harness the Stones' powers, and is shown in Avengers: Infinity War with the Stones embedded.

Love at First Sight
You see someone.

Hormones and neurochemicals from your brain flood your body.

Testosterone decreases in men and increases in women
(this may help men focus sexual interest)

3
ATTACHMENT
Increases with time in healthy and sustainable relationships, while limerence dwindles
Oxytocin (the "cuddle hormone") increases over time in long-lasting relationships

Love Can Be Scary
If you see someone you find attractive, your sympathetic nervous system may have a fight-or-flight reaction, releasing adrenaline, norepinephrine and cortisol.
Adrenaline and norepinephrine make your heart race.

Broken Heart?
Losing a loved one or a love interest can also cause a stress response in the body.
For older people, disruption in routine can cause depression.

Get the Nerve Up
Your vagus nerve runs from your brain to your stomach.
Your fight-or-flight stress response stimulates this nerve, which is why your stomach does somersaults when you see a person you find attractive.
