extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
COLOR

Profiles & Printing Explained

What's the Difference?
CMYK, RGB, and Pantone are industry standards for color profiles. How do they compare and what are their uses? Find out now!

CMYK

CMYK is the standard color profile for printing. Typically, your printer uses CYAN, MAGENTA, YELLOW, and BLACK inks to print colored images. When all colors are mixed together, an rich black is produced.

RGB

RGB refers to the three primary colors, RED, GREEN and BLUE. RGB colors use light to make their colors bright. If you mix them all together you would get pure white.

PANTONE

The Pantone Matching System (PMS) is used to correctly match materials with their corresponding RGB or CMYK colors. This insures that a color looks the same each time it is produced when matched to a Pantone swatch.

CMYK USE

CMYK is used for printed materials. CMYK colors do not appear as bright as RGB (they are missing the added bonus of a light source). Brochures, business cards, posters, etc. may be printed in CMYK.

RGB USE

RGB is the color profile used for digital screens and devices. Your screen mixes red, green, and blue light to produce the bright colors you see on your display. Websites, mobile apps, video, etc. are in RGB.

PMS USE

Pantone Matching System can be used to accurately match colors for anything from fabrics and paints to t-shirts and printed materials.

PANTONE®
247 C

Juice Co.

What About Printing?
Knowing the differences between the color profiles can help you to understand how different printing processes work and how those processes will affect your completed project.

SPOT COLOR (OFFSET)

+ =

PANTONE®
369 C

PANTONE®
350 C

Spot color printing describes the process where specialized ink or specially blended ink is created to make a perfect match that corresponds to your desired color, such as a Pantone swatch.

If there is just one color to be printed, there will be a single plate, and a single run of the press. If there are two colors, there will be two plates and two runs, and so on. The colors are layered onto the paper one by one.

BEST FOR:
Spot color printing would be typically used for jobs which require no full color imagery, such as for business cards and other stationery, or in monotone (or duotone etc) literature such as black and white newspaper print.

If you want something printed that has a unique color, like a metallic or fluorescent, spot color printing will be needed.

FOUR COLOR (OFFSET)

C M Y K =

4 Color process printing refers to the CMYK printing process. This means your artwork will be printed using only Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Black ink colors.

Just like spot color printing, each color is run through one at a time. The layering of each ink color produces a final product with a full color spectrum.

BEST FOR:
Four color process is the most common form of printing and is best for large batches, like 500 postcards. It produces clear, sharp prints.

* In some cases it may be possible to do a six-color or eight-color print. This type of process is more expensive, but will produce a wider range of colors.

DIGITAL PRINTING

Digital printing uses either toner or ink-jet technology. Digital printing might still use CMYK, but all colors are applied at once instead of with the "offset" roller method.

Although it has been considered in the past to not be as "crisp" as offset printing, technologies continue to improve and digital printing can produce some great results.

BEST FOR:
Digital printing is recommended for small batches, like 20 invitations or just one poster.

* Because digital printing does not require the creation of printing plates, turn-around time is often much faster.

Send individual, personal emails about your cause to folks, like legislators.

Blogging
- Ask local weekly newspapers or on-topic blogs if you can write a guest post on your cause.
- Begin your own blog for your cause, and become a resource for others.
- Email a link to your post to legislators and the cause’s PR people for more visibility.
- Inspire others to get fired up about your cause and possibly donate.

Facebook & Twitter
- Like the fan pages and accounts of causes you care about.
- Like and follow legislators and other people interested in these causes.
- When the causes post something you like, share it on your profile or retweet it.
- Tag legislators and others in posts about your cause so they’ll stay updated too.
- Search through Facebook’s causes app to connect with others interested in the same issue.

Investigating Data Scientists
Understanding their Skills, Roles and Team Makeup

A Study of 600+ Data Scientists*
Respondents provided self-assessment ratings about proficiency in 5 skill areas:
1. Business
2. Technology
3. Programming
4. Math
5. Statistics

Respondents provided information about their team makeup, job role, industry, satisfaction with work outcome, gender and more.

Most respondents were from B2B companies (80%), North America (64%). About 46% work for companies with less than 100 employees

*Results reflect data collected from AnalyticsWeek and Business of... Respondents completed surveys using AnalyticsWeek newsletter, blog posts, AnalyticsWeek and social media. (small print)

Top 5 Industries Employing Data Scientists
1. Information Technology (26%)
2. Education / Science (14%)
3. Consulting (13%)
4. Financial Services (11%)
5. Healthcare & Medical (9%)

Data Scientists are Highly Educated
Most data scientists hold graduate degrees (~67%)
Nearly half of data scientists hold a Master's degree
There was no difference between males and females in their educational attainment

Data Science Skills

Top 10 Data Science Skills
1. Communication
2. Structured Data
3. Data Mining and Visualization Tools
4. Math / Scientific Method
5. Statistics
6. Programming
7. Data Management
8. Machine Learning / Predictive Modeling
9. Product Design and Development
10. Business Development

Skill Areas of Data Science
Subject Matter
- Industry/domain expertise
- Business process knowledge
- Product management, marketing, operations knowledge
Programming & Technology
- Data collection, data cleaning, ETL, data engineering
- Databases, distributed computing, cloud computing, APIs
- Tools: R, Python, Java, Scala, SQL, Hadoop, Spark
Statistics
- Statistical modeling and inference
- Regression, classification, clustering, time-series
- Experimental design, A/B testing, causal inference

(Notes: radar/scatter/visualizations illustrating skill distributions; small footnote about survey methodology and definitions)

Data Science Roles

Four Types of Data Scientists
"Researcher" is the most popular data science job role

Business Managers include leaders, business persons and entrepreneurs
Developers include developers and engineers
Creatives includes Jack of all trades, artists and hackers
Researchers include scientists and statisticians

Data Scientists are not Created Equal
- Business Managers understand business (i.e., subject matter) but not tech or stats
- Developers understand technology and programming but not business or stats
- Creatives possess a basic knowledge in the three skill areas
- Researchers are strong in statistics and math but weak in business and programming

(visual: circular/radar chart showing strengths and weaknesses by role)

Data Science is a Team Sport

Diverse Data Science Teams Perform Better
Researchers and Developers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Business experts

Business Managers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Statistics experts

Bigger Teams, Bigger Outcomes
Data scientists who work with 4 or more people on their analytics projects report better outcomes compared to their counterparts who work with fewer people.

Getting Insights from your Data

The Scientific Method
1. Formulate a question or problem statement.
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable.
3. Gather / Generate data through experimentation or observation.
4. Analyze data / Draw conclusions (e.g., test hypotheses).
5. Communicate results / Take action (e.g., change processes) based on the conclusions. Refine hypotheses for further testing.

The scientific method is a way of investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge, or correcting and integrating previous knowledge.

(visual: cycle: Hypothesis -> Data -> Analysis -> Results/Conclusion)

Three Primary Skills of Data Scientists
(visual Venn diagram: Subject Matter, Statistics, Technology)
1. Subject Matter expertise helps you know what questions you need to ask.
2. Statistics expertise helps you apply the right analytics to answer your questions.
3. Technology expertise helps you access the data and process it quickly.

How to Apply Data Science Skills to the Scientific Method
(The scientific method helps data scientists organize their collective efforts to extract insight and value from data.)

1. Formulate a question or problem statement
   - Subject Matter
2. Generate a hypothesis / deductive guess
   - Subject Matter + Statistics
3. Gather / Generate data
   - Technology
4. Analyze / test hypotheses / draw conclusions
   - Statistics + Technology
5. Communicate results / Take action
   - Subject Matter + Communication

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
"Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?"
"Why should I settle if I can't afford the dream?"

The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
"How do I reach poor households?"
"Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?"

The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
"How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?"
"Why should we invest in this even riskier business?"

Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1
Stimulate Demand
• Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
• Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers' needs and preferences
• Offer flexible financing options

2
Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
• Build business capacity and market intelligence
• Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3
Improve the Investment Climate
• Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
• Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

question: What kind of Cholesterol is not harmful to your body?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

THE POWER OF BLOOD DIAGNOSTICS

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

70%

GOOD NEWS:

Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH

For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.

Approximately 41% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL

Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE HDL PRODUCTION:

Oatmeal
Fish
Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:

Cheese
Fried Foods
High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HDL/LDL LEVELS.

50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and Lp(a) gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

50%

TRIGLYCERIDES

A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:

Obesity
Cigarette Smoking
High-Carb Diet
Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION

A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS

Salmon
Blueberries
Oranges
Green Tea
Extra Virgin Olive Oil
Papayas
Sweet Potatos
Broccoli

THYROID

More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:

Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease.
Increase cold intolerance and fatigue.
Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy.

VITAMIN D

Because we are not outdoors and don't get as much sun as our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.

Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D; testing gives insight into how to achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST

OVERALL HEALTH
TIME

Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for the future.

WellnessFX™

SOURCES: THEVAPTEST.COM | NIH.GOV | RIGHTDIAGNOSIS.COM | IHEART.ORG | JOHNSHOPKINSHEALTHALERTS.COM | ADVAMED.ORG

On the Battlefield: Use of Drones in Combat

1 Oct. 7, 2001: The first armed Predator drones begin flying missions over Afghanistan less than a month after 9/11. By March 2002, Drones would begin providing close air support for troops on the ground.

2 Nov. 3, 2002: A CIA-operated Predator drone launched a Hellfire missile at a car carrying five al-Qaida members. It was the first drone assassination in what would become a decadelong campaign.

3 Dec. 23, 2002: A Predator drone fires a sidewinder missile at an Iraqi fighter plane in the No Fly Zone. This is the first instance of air-to-air combat with a drone. During the surge in 2007, drone surveillance would significantly reduce the number of roadside bomb attacks.

4 May 7, 2004: The CIA begins a covert drone bombing campaign in Pakistan's tribal regions. Over the next four years, President George Bush would order 42 more drone strikes in the region. From 2008 to 2011, President Barack Obama has ordered at least 180 strikes. Some estimates put the death toll from the campaign at close to 2,000.

5 Late 2004: The U.S. Border Patrol begins testing UAVs for patrolling the U.S./Mexico border. In 2009, the U.S. begins flying drones inside of Mexican airspace in support of Mexican government antidrug cartel operations.

6 Late 2009: U.S. begins drone flights off of Somalia to monitor and deter pirates. In June 2011, the U.S. begins basing drones at the Mogadishu airport and launching drone missile strikes within Somalia against al-Shabab militants linked to al-Qaida.

7 April 21, 2011: President Obama approves the use of drones in support of the Libyan rebels. The drones destroy Qaddafi air defenses in advance of the NATO bombing campaign, and provide targeting and support for NATO allies during the war. Operations in Libya also feature the first use of drone helicopters in combat.

8 Jan. 25, 2011: A Texas Department of Public Safety SWAT team deploys a small drone during a raid on a drug stash house. This marks the first use of UAVs for domestic surveillance.

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

-- The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.

-- Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.

-- The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness."

-- In the 4th century C.E, Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

-- ...and Christmas was born.

A Battle Won Before It Is Fought

Likely written during the violent and chaotic Warring States period of Chinese history (475–221 BCE), The Art of War offers a series of military strategies to wage war, involving such tactics as careful planning before battle. The strategies and tactics are analyzed and elaborated on by 13 commentators.

The Tao
Sun Tzu was influenced by Taoist philosophy, which emphasizes balance, virtuous choices, and harmony with nature.

Warring States Period
Sun Tzu lived during a turbulent period of military conflict among China's provinces.

Chinese War Literature
The Art of War was the first of the seven military classics in Chinese literature.

Key Figures

Li Ch'uan
T'ang dynasty writer of military strategy; prominent commentator

Ts'ao Ts'ao
Ruthless and effective King of Wei; senior commentator

Tu Mu
High-ranking government administrator; important commentator

Fooling the enemy into making miscalculations wins battles.

SUN TZU

question: Which social media site was invented in 2002?
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
friendster
THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platforms, every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING
Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?".

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT
ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately the letters "LO" weren't the complete "LOG" that they had planned to send over.

START OF SOCIAL SITES
From 1997 to 2001, Six Degrees had a peak of about 1 million members who were able to create profiles and "friend" each other.

1997

1999

CREATIVITY AND STATUS BLOGS
LiveJournal was a platform where users could blog as a form of keeping friends updated with their lives. It later became a format used by Facebook for their status updates.

A CHALLENGER EMERGES
Friendster, one of the first social media sites, is created and launched by Peter Chin, Jonathan Abrams and Suresh T. Within the next year they received 3 million users, widely considered one of the biggest blunders of Silicon Valley.

2002

2003

LINK UP WITH BUSINESSES
LinkedIn gave users a place to post their resume and private message people with a business-orientated mind. It's now a very popular site for users to search for employees and for people to search for new jobs. By 2004 the number of users on the site would reach 1 million, 3 years later they would break 500 million users.

2004

GMAIL RECEIVED
Google creates its own email service, Gmail, in January. In the UK Gmail was branded as Google Mail, due to a trademark dispute, until 2009 when they changed the branding back after the dispute was over.

FACEBOOK BEGINS
In February 2004, Facebook was launched by Mark Zuckerberg, along with his Harvard roommates. The site was initially limited to Harvard students but within the following months and years, this membership would be worldwide reaching the number of users to around 2.2 billion in 14 years.

2004

2005

BIRTH OF YOUTUBE
YouTube, a platform on which users can upload and share videos, was made public in February 2005. The first video to reach 1 million views was within this year thanks to a Nike advert featuring Ronaldinho. Facebook is now made available in the UK, Ireland, Canada, Australia and New Zealand on October 1st.

2006

MYSPACE IS BOOMING
Myspace became the most popular social network in the US during 2006! That's just 3 years after it was founded.

Twitter is launched publically on July 15th

750 TWEETS PER SECOND!
65 million tweets a day are being sent out on Twitter, that's around 750 tweets every second.

2010

2011

LAUNCH OF SNAPCHAT
Snapchat, an image messaging app, is launched in September after being created as an iOS only app known as Picaboo in July. One month later the app is pushed out before Snapchat is released.

SHORT VIDEOS, MEGA VIEWS
Vine, a video hosting service specialising in short videos, goes public in June. Twitter acquires Vine for $30 million in the October of that year.

2013

UK'S INSTA ADS
Instagram brings sponsored Ads to target the UK in September following their inclusion on the app in America.

2014

LIFE OF A STARMAN
NASA's #YearInSpace started, giving Twitter followers updates on Scott Kelly, an astronaut who lived in space for a year!

2015

VR AND FAKE NEWS
Virtual Reality, live videos, and 360-degree videos reigned all video content on social media sites like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram.
There was a crackdown on fake news throughout social media platforms like Facebook and Twitter.

2018

After all of this, we now have a beautiful array of social media channels to pick and choose from; Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Snapchat and more. We wonder what next advancement social media creators will think up. What do you think will happen in the future world of social media?

www.redpill.co

The social, environmental, and economic consequences could be devastating. Every region of the world would be affected, but the poor and most vulnerable will suffer the most. YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

THE POWER OF BLOOD DIAGNOSTICS

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

70%

GOOD NEWS:

Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH

For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.

Approximately 41% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL

Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE HDL PRODUCTION:

Oatmeal
Fish
Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:

Cheese
Fried Foods
High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HDL/LDL LEVELS.

50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and LPA(s) gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

50%

TRIGLYCERIDES

A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:

Obesity
Cigarette Smoking
High-Carb Diet
Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION

A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS

Salmon
Blueberries
Oranges
Green Tea
Extra Virgin Olive Oil
Papayas
Sweet Potatos
Broccoli

THYROID

More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:

Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease.

Increase cold intolerance and fatigue.

Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy.

VITAMIN D

Because we are not outdoors and don't get as much sun as our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.

Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D; testing gives insight into how you achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST

OVERALL HEALTH
TIME

Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for the future.

WellnessFX™

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach

tape your paper to the desk for extra stability

work on a slanted desk or a table if possible

give yourself plenty of room to work

don't keep your left elbow over the page - if you rest it on the paper you will smudge your work

make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

use your brush to transfer ink to your pen - brush a horizontal swipe of ink or gouache across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

adgh

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, mark up the line just below the bottom of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form a guide for measuring the size and height of your calligraphy letters. Put your pen nib against the paper and make a series of short vertical nib-width marks, stacked from the baseline up. This will give you a set of nib heights to measure with.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height is about 4–5 nibs high. Using your nib ladder, measure up from the baseline and draw a horizontal line to mark the x-height. This is the height of the main body of your lowercase letters.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure an additional 4–5 nibs above for the ascender line, and 4–5 nibs below the baseline for the descender line. These lines mark the tops and bottoms of letters like b, d, f, g, p, q, y.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. Use your nib measurements to set a cap-height above the x-height so your capitals are proportionate to your lowercase.

WARNING
If you're drawing your nib ladder with your bladder or your right hand, you'll find it tricky to be precise. Practice and take your time. Use a ruler to make sure your guide lines are straight. If your pen starts to drag or blot, wipe off excess ink and continue. Use a chisel-tip marker for practice if you want bolder guidelines.

BUSINESS ANALYTICS VS DATA SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.

To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS

Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS

Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

• Psychology

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master's degree in the field.

Decision-Making
Business Analysts
Instigate change by transforming business insights from other interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.

Data Scientists
Discover the opportunity in the datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making process.

Applied Problem Solving
Business Analysts
Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.

Data Scientists
Create the foundation of analysis by which an applied business problem can be solved.

Data Analysis
Business Analysts
Research, interpret, and visualize raw data using predictive, prescriptive and descriptive analysis to make it useful and accessible for business users.

Data Scientists
Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multinomial regression selection.

Analytical Models
Business Analysts
Understand, integrate, and prescribe solutions that use data modeling.

Data Scientists

What's your ideal workplace?

Many companies are just beginning to realize that workplace design directly impacts employee performance, yet research shows that 3 in 4 U.S. workers are not in optimal workplace environments.

Identify your workplace personality.

John Holland's Theory of Career Choice suggests that most people fit into six basic personality types in the workplace.

The Sellers
Enjoy leading, persuading, promoting and selling
Energetic, competitive and ambitious

The Organizers
Enjoy working with records, numbers, machines
Organized, orderly and precise

The Helpers
Enjoy activities that help people, such as teaching and healing
Friendly, supportive and trustworthy

The Artists
Enjoy creative activities such as art, writing and music
Imaginative, non-conformist, original and independent

The Thinkers
Enjoy studying, solving problems and working with information
Analytical, intellectual and scientific

The Doers
Enjoy working with tools and machines
Practical, mechanical and physical

Identify the ideal workplace.

The Open Office
This modern setup gathers employees in a large, open space. Without walls or cubicles, employees interact with each other frequently.
70% of U.S. workers go to offices with open floor plans.
Who should work here?
Allows privacy and ownership over workspace while still efficiently using square footage. (green check)
May decrease productivity due to constant noise and interruptions. (red x)

The Cellular Office
This traditional workspace is divided into individual offices, typically with doors for added privacy. Offices may be shared with more than one person.
76% of workers in cellular offices are very satisfied in their work environment.
Who should work here?
Offers more privacy, which is key for certain professions, while encouraging visual and social connections. (green check)
Not the most efficient use of space; some square foot closed doors result in less collaboration. (red x)

The Cubicle Setup
Between the open office design and the cellular office is the cubicle setup, which divides an open space into semi-private areas using medium to high walls.
90 ft2 is the average cubicle size, half the size of a typical executive office.
Who should work here?
Allows privacy and ownership over workspace while still efficiently using square footage. (green check)
Decreases employee communication (cubicle offices), while noise levels and distractions remain high. (red x)

Telecommuting
Thanks to advances in communication technologies, this increasingly popular work setup allows employees to work remotely either full- or part-time.
50% of U.S. workers hold a job that allows some telework.
Who should work here?
Saves thousands of dollars in overhead costs. Employees enjoy more flexibility, fewer interruptions and greater independence while staying more productive. (green check)
May result in isolation, lack of teamwork, and a loss of productivity, depending on the worker's home situation. (red x)

Coworking Space
Coworking spaces are shared office environments where remote workers can rent a workspace with access to printers, scanners and meeting space.
2x The number of coworking spaces worldwide doubles every year.
Who should work here?
Gives remote workers a chance to meet and network with a wider range of professionals. (green check)
Comes with its share of distractions and membership fees can be expensive versus working from home. (red x)

What's Next?
One size certainly does not fit all when it comes to workplace design. Progressive companies are experimenting with hot-desking (i.e. no assigned seating) and movable desks. Google has successfully adopted the idea of a hybrid workplace that features a wide range of spaces to suit various personalities and situations.

Craving some creative inspiration? Schedule a lunch meeting with a colleague, plan a brainstorming session, or opt for face-to-face conversations over emails.

question: What should you use to cover your lap, before you begin with your calligraphy?
ground_truths: ["old towels or cloths", "some old towels or cloths", "cover your lap with some old towels or cloths"]
extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your left wrist over the line you're drawing - if you rest it on the paper, you'll smudge your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

45°
hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

use your brush to add a horizontal stripe of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line across the bottom of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form and judge the size of your letters. With a piece of card cut to the width of your pen nib, make a set of steps by drawing lines across the page at intervals of one nib width. Use this ladder to mark out the heights for your letters.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height is about 4-5 nib heights. Using your nib ladder, measure 4-5 nib heights above the baseline and draw a line. This is the height of your lower case letters.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw the ascender and descender lines. The line above the x-height is your ascender line, the line below the baseline is your descender line.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It usually sits above the x-height and below the ascender line. Use your nib ladder to measure and draw the cap-height.

6 WARNING
If you're drawing with your nib holder in the wrong position, you'll have to try and correct your strokes afterwards. Work slowly and carefully while you learn. We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker before using your nib to get comfortable.

SOME LETTERS TO GET YOU STARTED

Hold your pen at a 30° angle for this alphabet

o c e

s i t

b d p

q g y

j u h

n m r

w v x a

f k z l

(Each letter illustrated with stroke numbers and directional arrows)

As Team USA is preparing for the Olympic Winter Games 2018, it is important to realize how The Games have changed over the years for Team USA. THEN & NOW

ENHANCED TRAINING WITH TECHNOLOGY FOR TEAM USA

1924
Early Days for Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games

Inability to monitor vitals or receive feedback during training

Could not learn from their practice runs, which made coaching a guessing game

Difficult to access medical history and past injuries slowed down recovery times

NOW
Technology Helps Prevent Injuries and Speeds Recovery for Team USA

Skin patches monitor heart rate, body temperature and sweat composition during training

Coaches learn from live feedback sent to a mobile app

Quick access to past medical records shortens recovery time

TEAM USA ATHLETES PRACTICE SMARTER, NOT HARDER

1936
First Alpine Skiing Event for Team USA

Trained the old-fashioned way — skiing down mountains

Warm weather locations a disadvantage, couldn't train year-round

No feel for the actual course beforehand

NOW
Team USA Can Now Simulate Ski Runs

Immersive video technology — Team USA skiers emulate any course

Athletes get to know the course without leaving the training facility

Immediate feedback allows for improvement

CHANGING THE WAYS WE WATCH TEAM USA

1960
Team USA's First Olympic Winter Games Broadcast on TV

13 hours of coverage over 11 days

NOW
Broadcast TV Not the Only Way to Watch Team USA

Livestream all events including Team USA at the opening and closing ceremonies

AFTER 30 YEARS, TEAM USA RETURNS TO SOUTH KOREA

A Look at Technology the First Time Team USA Competed in South Korea

New Technologies as Team USA Returns to South Korea

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
VISUAL HISTORY OF
Christmas Trees

The first Christmas tree was the centerpiece of holiday festivities in fifteenth century Latvia. Young men and women danced and sang around the tree before setting it on fire the last night of festivities. From those early traditions, to the first American tree in 1816, and into the present Christmas season, Christmas trees have been the focal point of holiday cheer. This timeline takes a look at some of the significant moments in the modern history of this Christmas icon.

Landmark trees
Decoration inventions
Christmas culture
Tree industry

1600
First decorated tree

1883
First artificial tree
Sears, Roebuck & Company began offering the first artificial Christmas trees -- 33 limbs for $.80 and 55 limbs for $1.00.

“It's not good to cut down trees for mere decoration,” says President Teddy Roosevelt in 1901, launching a century long debate on real vs. artificial trees.

1910
1910s Glass candleholders & light trees

Small electric lights are already invented, but are too expensive and dangerous for trees.

Overharvesting leads to evergreen shortages, leading to...

1920s Tree farms

The industry grows rapidly. By 2002, over 40 million trees are sold in the U.S. every Christmas.

1923 National Christmas Tree
The same large evergreen has been decorated every year since 1923. In 2011, the tree fell in a windstorm and a new one was planted.

1930
Brush company manufactures first bristle trees.

1933 Rockefeller Center
The tallest Christmas tree ever at Rockefeller was a 30 foot spruce in 1999.

1920s Feather trees
Imported from Germany, feather trees become popular in U.S. and range in size from 2 inches to 6 foot.

1940s Tree flocking
The West Coast reinvents Christmas with flocking kits that can be applied with vacuum cleaners.

1946 Bubble lights

1955 Disneyland Main Street Tree
Sixty foot tall live tree until 2008, when they switched to an artificial one for environmental reasons.

1958
Aluminum trees are first non-green artificial trees.

1957 How The Grinch Stole Christmas
Two classics that remind everyone it's the spirit of giving, not a tree, that makes Christmas.

1965 A Charlie Brown Christmas
Charlie's negative portrayal of artificial trees causes dip in sales.

1966 White House Blue Room Tree
National Christmas Tree Association begins presenting the White House with a tree from the annual Grand Champion award-winning growers.

1970–80s
Plastic trees lead to explosion of artificial trees as green alternative.

In 1992, 46% of homes used an artificial tree. The number jumped to 58% in 2004.

1980 Largest tree lit in Gubbio, Italy
8.5 kilometers of cable outlines a 650 meter tall tree.

1989 A Christmas Vacation
The Griswold Family's quest for the perfect tree is full of hilarious holiday disasters.

1990s Fiber-optic trees

2000s War on Christmas
Politicians debate whether it's more politically correct to refer to Christmas trees as 'holiday' trees.

Meanwhile, sales continue to boom. Over 17.4 million artificial trees were sold in 2007.

2001 Lifelike polyethylene trees
New consumer technologies drive a new era of Christmas tree decorations.

2011

Today, artificial trees range from lifelike to glamorous, complete with spinning motors, multi-colored lights, and polyethylene plastics.
